title: Effectiveness of Intra-Articular Injections of Sodium Hyaluronate or Corticosteroids for Intracapsular Temporomandibular Disorders: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
journal: Journal of Oral & Facial Pain and Headache
research_question: To assess the effectiveness of intra-articular injections of sodium hyaluronate (NaH) or corticosteroids (CS) for treatment of intracapsular temporomandibular disorders (TMD).
methods_summary: Systematic review and meta-analysis of single- or double-blinded randomized controlled trials (RCTs) comparing NaH or CS injections to each other or placebo for intracapsular TMD due to osteoarthritis and/or internal joint derangement.
Electronic searches of MEDLINE via PubMed, Web of Science, and Cochrane Library conducted on March 17, 2015, with an update on June 7, 2017.
Three reviewers independently extracted data and assessed risk of bias using Cochrane Handbook methods.

Meta-analyses performed for change in VAS pain (standardized difference in means) and number of patients with improved symptoms (risk ratios), using fixed- or random-effects models based on heterogeneity.
dataset: 7 RCTs (total 372 subjects, 282 F, 111 M) from initial 275 publications; 4 studies eligible for meta-analysis.
key_findings: Pooled results showed no significant difference in short- or long-term pain improvement with NaH compared to CS (short-term: SDM = -0.040, 95% CI = -0.395 to 0.315, P = .825; long-term: SDM = -0.058, 95% CI = -1.055 to 0.939, P = .909).
Number of responders to NaH was significantly more than placebo in one study (RR = 3.194, 95% CI = 1.357 to 7.518, P = .008), but not significantly higher than CS in another study.
Most included studies had unclear risk of bias; one had high risk of bias due to large dropouts.
extracted_insights: Intra-articular injections of NaH and CS show comparable effectiveness for pain relief in intracapsular TMD, with some evidence that NaH is superior to placebo. However, the evidence is limited by unclear risk of bias in most studies and small sample sizes, highlighting the need for further high-quality RCTs to determine optimal dosing and long-term safety.
